archetype: Real-Time Cryogenic Bladder Fatigue & Polar Moment of Inertia Shifter for Orbital Propellant Management
domain: In-Space Propellant Logistics and Deep-Space Cryogenic Fluid Dynamics
description: A deterministic control system that orchestrates the complex fluid sloshing and thermal stratification of superfluid liquid hydrogen in microgravity environments during long-duration coasting phases. It utilizes onboard high-speed accelerometers and capacitive cavity sensors to calculate the instantaneous polar moment of inertia, actively deploying ultrasonic vibration arrays and thermal dipsticks to prevent propellant boiling-off and ensure reliable engine re-ignition. The system must operate with hard real-time guarantees on a radiation-hardened processor, fusing physics-based CFD models with sensor telemetry to mitigate the 'slosh-induced tumbling' risk in vacuum conditions.
rules: MUST implement a deterministic Real-Time Operating System (RTOS) kernel capable of sub-100 microsecond interrupt latency to handle propellant slosh dynamics, as standard OS schedulers introduce unacceptable jitter.
MUST adhere to ECSS-Q-ST-80C standards for software reliability in space applications, requiring formal method verification (e.g., SPARK Ada) for all flight-critical path code.
MUST interface with legacy telemetry protocols such as CCSDS (Consultative Committee for Space Data Systems) AOS/TC for command uplink and downlink, ensuring rigid packet synchronization.

MUST utilize a radiation-hardened FPGA for the initial sensor fusion layer to offload heavy computational loads (like Fast Fourier Transforms of acoustic signals) before they hit the main CPU, preventing single-event upsets.
MUST implement a 'Safe Mode' logic that defaults to a passive thermal equilibrium state if dual-redundant sensor disagreement exceeds 0.5%, preventing catastrophic fuel venting due to sensor noise.
anti_patterns: DO NOT use standardized cloud-based machine learning models for real-time control decisions, as the inference latency and network dependency are incompatible with the 2-second light-speed delay limits and total network isolation of deep space.
DO NOT implement dynamic memory allocation (malloc/free) during flight operations, as memory fragmentation in a radiation environment can lead to silent data corruption or heap exhaustion.

DO NOT rely on 'event-driven' architectures for the primary fluid control loop; the physics of cryogenic sloshing is continuous and requires a fixed-frequency, synchronous control loop to maintain stability.
key_technical_challenges: Modeling and compensating for non-linear, multi-modal fluid slosh behavior in zero-g where surface tension dominates gravity, requiring real-time solution of the Navier-Stokes equations on embedded hardware.
Mitigating Single-Event Effects (SEE) and latch-ups in the sensor fusion algorithms caused by cosmic ray impacts, requiring triple-modular redundancy (TMR) voting logic in software.
Calibrating capacitive level sensors in the presence of micro-vibrations from spacecraft attitude control thrusters which create false-positive fluid level readings.

recommended_stack.language: SPARK Ada — Provides provably correct memory safety and determinism, essential for flight-critical avionics.
recommended_stack.framework: VxWorks 7 — A hard real-time operating system with a proven track record in NASA and ESA missions.
recommended_stack.database_engine: SQLite (in-memory, WAL mode) — Lightweight, ACID-compliant, and widely used in space telemetry logging where no network is available.
recommended_stack.cache: null — Hardware cache is managed by the CPU; software cache is avoided to prevent cache coherency issues in multi-core radiation-hardened processors.
recommended_stack.message_broker: null — Point-to-point message passing via shared memory or lock-free queues within the RTOS to ensure zero-latency communication.
special_services: CFD Reduced-Order Model (ROM) Solver for Slosh Dynamics
